title: Node 24, TypeScript 7, and amqplib 2.0.1 Contract · Compiler Case: Unknown Message Validation
summary: A service mixes callback and promise APIs, assumes publish returns a Promise, trusts TypeScript types as runtime validation, and exits before asynchronous close and confirms settle. Planned artifact: Add and verify the unknown message validation increment in typed amqplib lifecycle and boundary contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Treat delivered bytes, fields, properties, headers, and decoded payload as untrusted runtime data despite TypeScript declarations.
Choose one amqplib promise or callback API and account for RPC methods, event emitters, callback-only confirms, and non-RPC methods.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Predict Unknown Message Validation · Theory Unknown Message Validation
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory messaging team handles case 4879 in a recovery drill with stale callbacks from an old channel generation. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to gate a typed amqplib client without treating types or publish booleans as broker proof; bound payload, buffer, and retained-ledger bytes; then remove the dead-letter binding and expose the resulting retention risk. Browser code models pure messaging decisions only. amqplib, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, external effects, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Treat delivered bytes, fields, properties, headers, and decoded payload as untrusted runtime data despite TypeScript declarations.
